verses.commentary: They cross to Gentile territory, the region of the Gerasenes. Jesus invades pagan land. The kingdom expands beyond Jewish boundaries.
A demon-possessed man emerges from tombs. Death and demons intertwined. He lives among the dead, unclean by every measure.
No one could bind him, not even with chains. Superhuman strength. Demonic power breaks human restraints. Society's solutions fail.
Chains torn apart, irons broken. Repeated attempts, repeated failures. No one strong enough. Humanity is helpless against such evil.
Night and day he cries out, cuts himself with stones. Torment without relief. Self-destruction. The demonic drives toward death.
He sees Jesus from a distance, runs, falls on his knees. Compelled to approach. Demons are drawn to Jesus even as they resist him.

He shouts: What do you want with me, Jesus, Son of the Most High God? Full titles. Demons recognize what humans miss. In God's name don't torture me. Appeal to God for protection from God. Ironic, futile.
Jesus had already commanded: Come out. The encounter began before the narrative reveals it. Jesus initiated. The man's approach was response to Jesus' command.

What is your name? Jesus asks. My name is Legion, for we are many. A Roman legion was 6,000 soldiers. Massive demonic infestation. One man, many demons. Colonial imagery: the man is occupied territory.
The demons beg not to be sent out of the area. They plead repeatedly. Limited territory, desperate to remain. Even demons prefer some locations to exile.
A large herd of pigs nearby. Unclean animals in unclean land. Irony: Gentile space, but still under demonic dominion.
Demons beg: Send us into the pigs. Desperate negotiation. They need permission. Jesus' authority absolute.

Jesus permits. Demons enter pigs. Two thousand pigs rush into the lake and drown. Visual demonstration of demonic intent: destruction. What they did to the man, they do to the pigs. Death is their goal.
Pig herders report to town and countryside. News spreads. People come to see. Curiosity, not faith.

They find the man sitting, dressed, in his right mind. Complete transformation. From naked, screaming, self-destructive to clothed, calm, rational. And they were afraid. Restoration terrifies them more than possession did.
Eyewitnesses explain: the man, the pigs. Economic loss made explicit.

They plead with Jesus to leave. Astonishing. Divine power displayed, a man restored, and they want Jesus gone. They prefer demons to Jesus. Economic concerns trump human restoration. The cost of the kingdom is too high.
The healed man begs to go with Jesus. Natural response. He wants to stay near his deliverer.

Jesus refuses. Go home, tell your people what the Lord has done, how he showed mercy. Reverse of the messianic secret. In Gentile regions, Jesus sends a herald. The man is the first missionary to the Decapolis.
The man proclaims in the Decapolis (ten Gentile cities) what Jesus did. All were amazed. Obedience leads to impact. Testimony spreads the kingdom.
Jesus crosses back to the Jewish side. Crowds gather again. The pattern resumes.
Jairus, a synagogue leader, comes and falls at Jesus' feet. A respected official humbles himself. Desperation drives even the powerful to Jesus.
My little daughter is dying. Come and put your hands on her so she will live. Faith mixed with urgency. He believes Jesus must be physically present to heal.
Jesus goes with him. Crowds press around. Ministry interrupted, as always.
A woman with bleeding for twelve years. Chronic, debilitating condition. Ritually unclean (Leviticus 15:25–27). Socially isolated.
She suffered under many doctors, spent all she had, grew worse. Medical failure. Financial ruin. Desperation compounds.
She hears about Jesus, comes behind him in the crowd, touches his cloak. Stealth approach. Her uncleanness makes her touch illegal. She risks rebuke, even punishment.
She thinks: If I just touch his clothes, I will be healed. Faith operates on what she has heard. Touch is enough. She believes his power is transferable.
Immediately the bleeding stops. She feels the healing in her body. Instant awareness. Twelve years of suffering end in a moment.
Jesus realizes power has gone out from him. He turns: Who touched my clothes? He knows. The question is pedagogical. He will not let her disappear in the crowd.
Disciples incredulous: everyone is touching you in this crowd. Logical objection. Jesus' question seems absurd given the press of bodies.
Jesus keeps looking. He will find her. The healing is not enough; relationship is the goal.
The woman, trembling, comes forward. Fear overwhelms her. She tells the whole truth. Public confession. Her secret healing becomes public testimony.
Daughter, your faith has healed you. He calls her daughter. Intimacy, acceptance. Go in peace, be freed from your suffering. Full restoration: physical, social, spiritual. Shalom granted.
Messengers arrive: Your daughter is dead. Why bother the teacher? Delay has cost the girl's life. Implied: too late now. Jesus can heal, but not resurrect. Their theology is too small.
Jesus overhears, tells Jairus: Don't be afraid; just believe. Command and invitation. Fear and faith oppose. The interruption was not a mistake.
Jesus allows only Peter, James, and John to accompany him. Inner circle. Some miracles are too sacred for crowds.
They arrive at Jairus' home. Commotion, crying, wailing. Mourning has begun. Death is certain.
Why the commotion? The child is not dead but asleep. Jesus redefines the situation. Sleep, not death. For him, death is temporary.
They laugh at him. Scorn. They know death when they see it. Jesus puts them all out. Takes only parents and the three disciples. Unbelief excluded.
He takes her hand, says Talitha koum (Aramaic preserved). Little girl, get up. Simple command. Direct address to the dead.
Immediately she stands and walks. She was twelve years old (same number of years as the woman's illness). Complete restoration. They are astonished. Life from death.

Jesus orders silence. Messianic secret again. Tell no one. But give her something to eat. Practical care follows miracle. Resurrection does not eliminate ordinary needs. Chapter five demonstrates Jesus' authority over demons, disease, and death. Nothing is beyond his reach.
